data.text:
, 

MV: [INAUDIBLE]. 

INT: Part three, take one. Running tape 13. 19th of May, 1997. Interview with Mark Verstandig. Last tape we were talking about the beginning of your period working in [NON-ENGLISH] as-- officially as a lawyer. 

MV: Yes, it was-- it was almost officially. As a matter of fact, at that time, as I mentioned before, employing a Jewish lawyer was penalized with death. Even as a-- even as a cleaner, a Jewish lawyer couldn't have been employed by a-- by a-- by a Polish lawyer. 

And interestingly, may I tell you that in Warsaw, in Warsaw, the head of the bar was a well-known antisemite, a lawyer who was known from before the war, that he tried to exclude Jewish lawyers from practicing law in Poland. 

But when the Germans issued this decree, that a-- a Polish lawyer should not employ a Jewish lawyer, the Jewish lawyer has no right to-- to practice law, he-- the same-- the same fellow-- I don't remember his name-- he said, no. This is not on our agenda now. We will not going to accept such a discrimination against our Jewish colleagues. 

And he employed a Jew, or-- or some Jews, and so did others. And soon the Germans cracked down. And he and a number of-- of Polish lawyers-- I think about 80 or 90-- were sent to Auschwitz. At that time, Auschwitz was not as dangerous as a year later because some of them came back. 

There was still no gas chambers at that time at Auschwitz, but it was a very dangerous concentration camp. And many times within a few weeks, the ashes came back. At that time, the administration of Auschwitz were-- played-- played a game. They were sending back-- even the Jews-- sending back the ashes that-- they regret very much that the prisoner such and such died of pneumonia, natural causes. 

Well, I'm pointing it out that at that time, there were-- the Polish antisemites in Warsaw, the lawyers, were defying the German-- the German order not to employ Jews. But later, they stopped, after-- after about 80 or 90 lawyers were sent to Auschwitz, that was the end of it. 

In Mielec, it was different. It was a small town. And I must have been the only lawyer in Poland practicing law. I used to go every day to court. When I came to court and I entered the courthouse, I used to take off my-- this white band. It was this-- star of David. 

And the judge, if I was representing, would say the-- the-- the defendant or the plaintiff. And the judge [INAUDIBLE] would say to-- to the minutes, on behalf of the plaintiff of [NON-ENGLISH] Droba. That was the Pole, my senior partner. So as far as the minutes are concerned, it was Droba and not me. 

And that went on for almost to the time of-- to the time of the expulsion of Jews, of the liquidation of the-- of the Jewish community [INAUDIBLE]. At that time, may I tell you, that I was, otherwise, very friendly with the judges. And there were a few judges who were excellent lawyers and they enjoyed just discussions on law, like, in-- in the yeshiva [INAUDIBLE]. 

Just kind of a [INAUDIBLE] and so because Poland had two or three different-- three different laws. There was the old law, the Russian law, the German law, the [INAUDIBLE] law, and later, the Polish law, which-- the-- the new Polish law. But some still existed before, and we used to discuss the differences in the wording and so on. 

I was very friendly with them. And a very interesting-- a very interesting incident developed, which had some bearings later, very important ones. The number of judges in Mielec were the same as before the war, but the cases were very few. Very, very, very, very-- there were fewer cases than-- than-- than before the war. 

And there was a [INAUDIBLE] that the [INAUDIBLE] department-- the-- the-- the department-- the Justice Department [INAUDIBLE], of Dr. [INAUDIBLE], will dismiss some judges, and they sent them in to the concentration camp. Of course, if [INAUDIBLE], you wouldn't be nice. The best-- the best way was to-- to send them to Auschwitz. 

They sent them to a concentration camp. And we discussed this matter, the danger to the judges. And then I came up, privately [INAUDIBLE] in the chambers to discuss it with-- with the Judge Kosowski. I came up with an idea. 

I told them, look. [INAUDIBLE] the-- the Justice Department, [INAUDIBLE] Justice Department is looking at the numbers of cases, but not at the cases itself. What about if I supplied, every day, a number of fictitious cases? 

The procedure is such. You supply a case, and then the court asked you to-- ask you to pay the fee within seven days. If you don't pay the fee, the case-- you-- the whole case goes [NON-ENGLISH], to the archive. And there it lies for three years. And after three years, it's out. It's off. 

So I told them, look. We will supply, every day a number of cases. They will get numbers, current numbers in the index of cases [INAUDIBLE] were issued. And you will ask-- you will ask us to supply the money to supply the fee. We will not supply it within seven days. 

So it will go as [NON-ENGLISH], but the number will be there. And they said-- it was a conference of the judges-- it's an excellent idea, and-- but you shouldn't spread it all over. I-- we will be-- our-- our office will be the only ones supplying those cases. And we used to supply 10, 20 cases a-- a day. Didn't cost us nothing. 

Didn't cost us-- couldn't cost us anything. They ask for money. And as far as the Jews are concerned, there was still another-- another point, namely, if a Jew issued a writ or a Jew was sued, a translation of that writ should be submitted in German and sent to the government, to the-- to-- to-- to-- to the [NON-ENGLISH], to the-- to the Justice Department. 

The reason for that was that the Jews had to submit a list of all their-- of all-- of-- of all their assets, including-- including claims to the Justice Department, so that they should know the-- all the jewelry, everything what they had, so they should know how much the Jew is worth. 

Now, there was a-- there was a kind of a fear-- the Germans feared that the Jew may cover his assets by submit-- by asking a Pole to claim his assets. And then, he will not defend it, and the assets will be transferred to the Pole and-- a trusted Pole-- and they will keep it for him. 

Well, the Germans wanted to know about it. On the other hand, if a Jew choose somebody else, another Jew or a Pole, the government wanted to know-- the German government wanted to know whether this claim was submitted beforehand. 

If it was not submitted, they will immediately seize it. And they will enter as a plaintiff against the Pole. Well, that was a kind of-- of controlling whether the Jew-- whether the Jew submitted all his-- a list of all his assets. 

Now, what they wanted is-- whether a Jew was a defendant or-- or-- or a plaintiff, a translation of the writ should have gone-- should have been submitted to the German government. So many times, when I-- when I ran out of names, I used to submit-- I used to submit Jewish names, and I used to be myself sometimes as a plaintiff. 

For instance, I-- I issued a writ against Mr. so and so that before the war, I lent him 100 zloty, and he didn't' return it to me. Therefore, I ask the court to issue a writ that he should return me the 100 zloty with interest. I should have submitted the-- a translation, but I did not. I should have paid the fee. I did not. 

And the judge, the court ask-- the court ask-- the office of-- of Droba to submit a list, submit a translation, and to pay the fees. We did not, and it went to [NON-ENGLISH]. And so it went on. 

The court of Melbourne-- the court of Melbourne was working on a-- on-- on-- on a number of nonexistent-- of nonexistent cases. And then something happened. Namely, there was a-- a colleague of mine from the same high school, [INAUDIBLE] Greenberg-- was the family Greenberg. He finished the high school. He didn't study, wasn't a scholar. But he somehow learned the trade of making artificial wine. 

You could make wine, artificial wine, made out of yeast, sugar, and essence of wine, from the same-- you-- you-- you pour it into a-- into a large barrel, and poured in water, a certain amount of yeast. That was the secret, a certain amount of yeast. And a certain amount of sugar. 

The yeast brought about the-- the fermentation. The-- the sugar brought about the fermentation-- the fermentation of the yeast, and the yeast, the fermentation of the sugar. And the sugar changed into alcohol. And later, after a certain-- after a certain amount of days, the alcohol was-- there was enough alcohol already. I mean, the whole sugar was turned into alcohol with the-- with the-- with the yeast. 

And then was a question of-- of coloring and the question of a certain essence to make it Bordeaux, or to make it a Mosel, whatever you wanted. From the same barrel, you could have a red wine and you could have white wine. That used to be a Jewish trade for many, many centuries. 

Somehow this-- the-- the brother of this fellow, Greenberg, marries into a such a family, and thus he didn't go to university. He went to his brother, he works in his factory, and he learns a trade. And then he came back to [NON-ENGLISH] and there he engaged-- he made the-- he-- he made a company-- he makes company with a former teacher, a professor of gymnasium, [INAUDIBLE] a certain Mr. Zbreschavski, Professor Zbreschavski. And he opened his factory making wine. 

And they didn't have [INAUDIBLE] capital, so they found another Jew, a certain Mr. Leipzig. And he sold his jewelry and [INAUDIBLE], and he put in the money. And so, it was a company made by Leipzig, Greenberg, and Zbreschavski, and they started-- they started to make artificial wine. 

And after a while, after a good few months, they were producing thousands of liters of wine daily. And German lawyers used to take away the wine and pay the money. It was very difficult to recognize, but when a Mosel from this barrel then a Mosel from-- from-- from a German-- from-- from a German winery. 

Anyhow, and that went on for a good few months until one day this Mr. Leipzig came to the-- came to work. He worked [INAUDIBLE]. And Mr. Greenberg, that is the friend of my-- my-- my-- my colleague from the gymnasium, told him, look. I think we don't need you anymore. You can go. 

Said, what do you mean you don't need me anymore? I'm a partner. He said, you are not a partner. You are a worker. Out you go. And he called in another couple of Poles, and they threw him out. In [NON-ENGLISH], he knew that the [NON-ENGLISH] could hear the conversation because he was in the other room. 

When he went, [INAUDIBLE] Zbreschavski, and Zbreschavski said to him, look. I'm sorry. I don't know anything about you. The manager of this factory is coming back. He's-- and he said-- and he said to me this morning that he doesn't need you anymore. 

He said, but I'm a partner. I don't anything about that. He just pushed him away. But this company, of the-- the partnership, was [INAUDIBLE] in a contract. They came because they couldn't go to a-- to a non-Jewish lawyer, but they went to Dr. [NON-ENGLISH], who didn't work as a lawyer. I-- as a matter of fact, I-- I-- it was before the war, and after the war, the moment I got some money to pay for it, I was living in their flat, [INAUDIBLE]. 

And he prepared this document that they are partners. And if there is a dispute between them, it will be resolved in an arbitration. So this fellow came to me and asked me if I could help. He has got the document that he's a partner. I told him, yes. I think I could. We'll ask Droba if he's willing to accept a case like this. 

I talked to Droba. Droba says, why not? So we wrote a letter to this Greenberg and to Zbreschavski that our client submitted a case to us. And there's a-- should be an arbitration. So he's nominating [NON-ENGLISH] Droba as his arbitrator. And we ask him-- we ask them to submit another arbitrator on their behalf. 

And if they can't resolve, they will choose [INAUDIBLE]. And if they can't resolve this [INAUDIBLE], they will go to court, as if this-- as-- as-- according with the law. And we want an answer within seven days. Surely, they didn't answer. 

But on the seventh day, when I went down from work, Mr. Greenberg waited for me, and he [INAUDIBLE]. He put his arm around me and said, look. We received this letter. And we threw it out, just in the rubbish bin. And we would like to ask you to keep your fingers clean out of this matter. 

You know, you're playing with fire. We are supplying the German army. I didn't say anything. But went to [INAUDIBLE]-- to Droba, and we wrote him another letter. And we asked him that-- which was, by the way, hand delivered. And we asked him that within 24 hours, we wanted a reply whether or not they are going to. 

Now, they didn't-- they didn't answer, so we issued a writ to the courts. It said, issued a writ that we-- that this is in accordance with the law, that the-- that the court should nominate an arbitrator on their behalf. That is the law. So the writ was not for anything more, but that the court should-- should-- that the court should nominate an arbitrator. 

Now, the question was, at that time, of a-- of a translation, if we put a translation in. Within seven days, the German government ruled-- immediately put-- [INAUDIBLE] confiscates the whole business and put Greenberg and Zbreschavski into jail. 

So we asked-- I asked Kosowski that he should give us time to submit a translation, not within seven days, but in 30 days. And the reason being that that is not a writ for a claim, that the writ is from an arbiter, whether there is a claim or not, we still don't know. After the [INAUDIBLE], after the-- after the arbitration court will issue-- will issue the judgment, then before we-- before we give it to-- before the court will acknowledge it and send it to the sheriff, then we will issue. 

They-- they agreed with me that it is [INAUDIBLE] times or 30 days, and we issued the writ against them. Now after a couple of weeks, they came along, made an appointment, came along with Dr. [INAUDIBLE]. He was a very nice man, very nice man. They came along to negotiate, and we started negotiations. 

In the discussion somehow this-- this Professor Zbreschavski, who used to be a very nice and-- a very nice and decent-- decent fellow before the-- he somehow said to Droba, [INAUDIBLE]. That's what you have done with this writ. It's blackmail. And before the war, I will make you accountable for that. 

Droba jumped up, opened the door. He said, you [INAUDIBLE]. You made an agreement with another Jewish fellow thinking that because he is Jewish, he will cover you up. But you are both highway robbers. And under the-- and-- and under the protection of the German law, you wanted to rob this fellow. You robbed this fellow and threw him out. And I've got a document for it. You are telling me this, and he said, now, get out of my-- of my office before I kick you out, or before I just give you a kick out of it. And he threw him out. 

To make a long story short, within another 10, 12 days, before the-- before the-- the matter was finished, they came along, they paid some money. They paid a hell of a lot of money to this fellow. They paid him back, and they paid him-- they-- they paid him a part of-- of the profits. 

And I knew that the only thing they will ask will be to return them the letter, the contract, which [NON-ENGLISH] produced, that he's a partner. Because after the war, they will say that it was blackmail. Now, before-- I knew that he will go-- that he ask him. But when we went-- we went to a-- to a-- to a-- photographer, trusted photographer, [NON-ENGLISH] and he made-- there was no-- there was no photostat, copy machine. And he made a photo out of that. And they came, and he sent in Dr. [NON-ENGLISH], and they want it back. 

And so we gave them back-- we gave them back the document, and they paid some money. But that was not the end of the case. A week later, I was in court during the morning. A Gestapo fellow came in. He wanted to discuss some of this with Dr. Droba. 

Dr. Droba invited him to his office. And he immediately said to the secretary-- a Jewish secretary was there. He said, Frieda, and sent her to-- just to disappear, because he's not allowed to employ-- but she didn't look Jewish. 

So she disappeared. She disappeared, and they were sitting in his office [INAUDIBLE] in-- in-- in-- in his office with the Gestapo. The Gestapo told him, look. I've got a report that a certain Dr. Mark Verstandig is working with you. And that is against the law. 

He said, no. He doesn't work here. He comes here from time to time. We have a conversation. We are good friends. We have a conversation, but he doesn't work here. Now, while he was talking, somebody opens the door. There was nobody in there-- in-- in the first floor where the receptionist was sitting-- of course, she disappeared. 

So he went through, opens the door, opens the door, looked in, and saw Droba. He didn't see the Gestapo man. And he said, can I speak to Dr. Verstandig? And he said to him, Dr. Verstandig doesn't-- doesn't work here. What do you want Dr.-- what do you mean he don't work here? I was yesterday here. I gave him a case. 

INT: Thank you. We'll continue next tape. 

MV: Huh? 

INT: We'll continue next tape.
